name: scvi-tools (Deep Learning for Single-Cell Omics)
pricing_notes: Open source (BSD-3-Clause); Yosef Lab / UC Berkeley

auth.notes: Local library; scvi-hub model downloads may require HuggingFace token

performance.notes: PyTorch + GPU; variational training on 100K+ cells; GPU strongly recommended

agent_readiness.notes: Deep generative models for single-cell omics. import scvi. scvi.model.SCVI.setup_anndata(adata, layer='counts', batch_key='batch'). model = scvi.model.SCVI(adata, n_layers=2, n_latent=30). model.train(). latent = model.get_latent_representation(). normalized = model.get_normalized_expression(). de_results = model.differential_expression(groupby='leiden'). scANVI for semi-supervised cell annotation. totalVI for CITE-seq. scARCHES for transfer learning. scvi-hub for pretrained models.

gotchas: GPU strongly recommended — CPU training is very slow
Raw counts in adata.layers['counts'] required — not normalized
Training is stochastic — results vary between runs
Batch correction assumes a linear batch effect — may not suit all datasets
best_when: Batch correction and data integration across multiple scRNA-seq experiments
Variational autoencoder latent space for UMAP (replaces PCA)
CITE-seq (RNA + protein) joint analysis with totalVI
Semi-supervised cell type annotation with scANVI
avoid_when: Small datasets (<1000 cells) where simpler methods work
Without GPU — training will be too slow
When interpretability is required (black box VAE)
summary: scvi-tools provides deep generative models for single-cell omics. SCVI.setup_anndata(adata, layer='counts'). model.train(). get_latent_representation(). differential_expression(). scANVI for annotation. totalVI for CITE-seq. BSD-3 by Yosef Lab/UC Berkeley. Best for batch correction and data integration across experiments. Raw counts required. GPU needed. scvi-hub for pretrained foundation models.
